target.title: feat(llm): add Inworld as built-in llm provider
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

Inworld is currently a TTS-only plugin in OpenClaw. Users cannot use Inworld as an LLM provider for chat completions, despite Inworld offering an OpenAI-compatible LLM API with routing, tool calls, and reasoning.

Why does this matter now?

TTS integration landed in #55972. LLM completes the provider, making Inworld a single-key solution for both speech and inference.

What is the intended outcome?

`openclaw onboard --auth-choice inworld-api-key` configures Inworld as an LLM provider; `inworld/auto` resolves via live catalog discovery and completes against `https://api.inworld.ai/v1/chat/completions`. Streaming, tool calls, and per-model reasoning levels all work out of the box.

What is intentionally out of scope?

Custom base URL override for the LLM surface, multi-key rotation, embeddings/image/video/STT endpoints, UI onboarding graphics, surfacing Inworld's response `metadata` (routing diagnostics) back into openclaw cost attribution.

What does success look like?

Live catalog (`/llm/v1alpha/models`) populates on authenticated startup, completions (streaming + non-streaming + tool calls) work, per-model reasoning levels map correctly to openclaw effort ids, existing TTS behavior is unchanged.

What should reviewers focus on?

Plugin manifest shape additions, catalog discovery caching strategy (process-lifetime, no TTL polling), `normalizeResolvedModel` wire-id rewrite for first-party `inworld/models/<NAME>` refs, and `EFFORT_*` → openclaw effort id mapping with per-model `supportedLevels`.

<details>
<summary>Summary guidance</summary>

This PR description is the contributor's durable explanation of the change. Write it for human maintainers first; ClawSweeper and Barnacle use the same text to understand intent, proof, risk, and current review state.

</details>

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Does not close any open issue.

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related: #55972 (TTS integration this PR extends). Implementation pattern mirrors the OpenRouter and Chutes provider plugins (live `/llm/v1alpha/models` discovery + small static fallback).

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

Inworld team follow-up to the merged TTS PR #55972 to complete the provider integration.

<details>
<summary>Linked context guidance</summary>

Link the issue, PR, discussion, maintainer request, or owner request that explains why this PR should exist.

</details>

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Inworld is now selectable as an LLM provider; chat/stream/tool calls succeed against `api.inworld.ai/v1/chat/completions`.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS 25.5.0, Node 24.14, local OpenClaw checkout at the PR head SHA, real Inworld API key against `api.inworld.ai`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  ```bash
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/inworld
  curl -sS -X POST -H "Authorization: Basic $INWORLD_API_KEY" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
       -d '{"model":"<wire-id>","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Reply OK only."}],"max_tokens":16}' \
       https://api.inworld.ai/v1/chat/completions
  ```
- **Evidence after fix (live console capture from this branch checkout):**

  ```
  === pnpm test extensions/inworld ===
   RUN  v4.1.7 /Users/.../public/openclaw
   Test Files  6 passed (6)
        Tests  49 passed (49)
     Duration  3.47s

  === Live POST /v1/chat/completions (5 representative models) ===
    OK   inworld/models/GLM-5.1            -> wire-model=inworld/GLM-5.1                          finish=stop credential=system
    OK   anthropic/claude-opus-4-8         -> wire-model=anthropic/claude-opus-4-8                finish=stop credential=system
    OK   openai/gpt-4o                     -> wire-model=openai/gpt-4o                            finish=stop credential=system
    OK   google-ai-studio/gemini-2.5-flash -> wire-model=google-ai-studio/gemini-2.5-flash        finish=stop credential=system
    OK   auto                              -> wire-model=deepinfra/deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V4-Flash  finish=stop credential=system
  ```

  Each `wire-model=...` row is the exact `model` field Inworld echoed back, confirming the plugin emits canonical wire ids — including the rewrite from user-facing `inworld/models/GLM-5.1` to wire form `inworld/GLM-5.1`, and Inworld's routing selection for `auto`.

  Separate live catalog probe: `GET /llm/v1alpha/models` returned 124 entries; iterating each through `parseInworldModel` produced 124 valid `ModelDefinitionConfig` results and 0 parse errors.

- **Observed result after fix:** Every probed user ref reaches `api.inworld.ai/v1/chat/completions` and returns a valid OpenAI-shaped response with `finish_reason: "stop"`. First-party id rewrite is observable in the `wire-model` echo.
- **What was not tested:** Windows/Linux runtime (only macOS), interactive `openclaw onboard` wizard end-to-end through the new auth choice, prompt-cache billing reconciliation against Inworld's invoice surface, full coverage of every reasoning-effort level on every model, every one of the 124 catalog entries individually via chat completions (probed 13 representative models, one per upstream provider).
- **Proof limitations or environment constraints:** Live tests require a valid `INWORLD_API_KEY` with LLM-router access. CI will only run the offline vitest layer without the env var; the gated live test auto-skips when `OPENCLAW_LIVE_TEST` is unset or the key is missing.
- **Before evidence:** Before this PR, `openclaw onboard --auth-choice inworld-api-key` did not exist; `inworld/<provider>/<model>` refs were unresolvable; `openclaw models list` did not list any Inworld LLM entries. TTS via `messages.tts.providers.inworld` continued to work and is unchanged by this PR.

<details>
<summary>Real behavior proof guidance</summary>

External contributors must show after-fix evidence from a real OpenClaw setup. Unit tests, mocks, lint, typechecks, snapshots, and CI are supplemental only.

</details>

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/inworld
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.bundled.config.ts
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.extensions.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions.tsbuildinfo
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo
node --import tsx scripts/generate-config-doc-baseline.ts --check
node --import tsx scripts/check-import-cycles.ts
node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs extensions/inworld
INWORLD_API_KEY=<your-key> node scripts/test-live.mjs -- extensions/inworld/inworld.live.test.ts
```

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- `extensions/inworld/models.test.ts` — catalog parser: pricing scale, modality detection, vision flag, `isSupported: false` rejection, missing-field handling, first-party id storage (no `inworld/` provider prefix), wire-id rewrite, `supportedLevels` capture, prompt-caching flag.
- `extensions/inworld/thinking-policy.test.ts` — `EFFORT_*` and lowercase short-form acceptance (forward-compat), per-model `supportedLevels` profile derivation, `medium`-preferred-else-highest default-level logic.
- `extensions/inworld/onboard.test.ts` — allowlist + primary-model side-effects of `applyInworldConfig`.
- `extensions/inworld/index.test.ts` — LLM provider registration alongside speech, fallback-catalog exposure, dynamic resolve for known/unknown ids.
- `extensions/inworld/inworld.live.test.ts` — gated live cases for non-streaming chat, SSE streaming, and tool calling.

What failed before this fix, if known?

N/A — new feature.

If no test was added, why not?

Tests added; see above.

<details>
<summary>Testing guidance</summary>

List focused commands, not every incidental check.

</details>

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? `Yes` — `inworld` now appears in `openclaw models list` as an LLM provider; `openclaw onboard --auth-choice inworld-api-key` becomes a valid auth choice; `agents.defaults.model.primary` accepts `inworld/<ref>`. Existing TTS behavior is unchanged.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? `Yes` — `extensions/inworld/openclaw.plugin.json` adds `providers`, `providerAuthChoices`, `modelIdNormalization`, `providerEndpoints`, `providerRequest`. `INWORLD_API_KEY` env var was already declared by the TTS PR; no new env vars introduced. `docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256` regenerated. `extensions/inworld/package.json` is intentionally untouched (npm package name stays `@openclaw/inworld-speech` even though the plugin now serves LLM + TTS).

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? `No` — same `INWORLD_API_KEY` and HTTP Basic scheme already shipped for TTS, same `fetchWithSsrFGuard` policy scoped to `api.inworld.ai`. No new outbound hosts; no new secret stores. The LLM secret target is auto-covered by the existing generic `models.providers.*.apiKey` wildcard registry entry.

What is the highest-risk area? `normalizeResolvedModel` rewrites first-party model ids (`models/<NAME>` → `inworld/models/<NAME>`) at the wire layer. A bug here would silently send the wrong `model` field to Inworld and break only first-party model requests.

How is that risk mitigated? Pure unit test (`toInworldWireModelId`) asserts the transform in both directions (first-party rewritten, passthrough refs untouched). Live test exercises the catalog-driven path for `inworld/auto` against `api.inworld.ai`. Additional live curl probe verified the first-party id rewrite end-to-end (see `inworld/models/GLM-5.1 -> wire-model=inworld/GLM-5.1` row in the proof block above). Hook only fires for ids starting with `models/`, so non-first-party refs are zero-impact.

<details>
<summary>Risk guidance</summary>

Use this for author judgment that is not obvious from the diff.

</details>

## Current review state

What is the next action? Initial review by maintainers and automated reviewers (ClawSweeper, Codex, greptile).

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof? Nothing on author side at submission. Open to feedback on the manifest shape and live-discovery caching strategy if reviewers prefer a different pattern than OpenRouter/Chutes.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed? Reverted `extensions/inworld/package.json` (kept original `@openclaw/inworld-speech` name) to avoid the dependency-guard CI gate; added pasted live console output to the Real behavior proof section so it isn't pure narration.

<details>
<summary>Review state guidance</summary>

Keep this as the durable state for review progress.

</details>

target.title: feat(sessions): auto-prune orphan store pointers during periodic main…
target.body:
…tenance

When transcript .jsonl files are removed (by archive cleanup, disk-budget enforcement, or external tooling) without clearing the corresponding pointer in sessions.json, those pointers become orphans. Inbound resolution cannot match them and the affected channel goes dormant.

This change wires the existing pruneMissingTranscriptEntries function into the periodic auto-maintenance path of updateSessionStore, gated by a new session.maintenance.fixMissing config flag (default: false, opt-in for one release while we collect telemetry).

Changes:
- src/config/types.base.ts: add SessionMaintenanceConfig.fixMissing
- src/config/sessions/store-maintenance.ts: extract pruneMissingTranscriptEntries here (was in cleanup-service.ts) and add fixMissing to ResolvedSessionMaintenanceConfig with default false
- src/config/sessions/cleanup-service.ts: re-import pruneMissingTranscriptEntries from store-maintenance.ts instead of defining it locally
- src/config/sessions/store.ts: call pruneMissingTranscriptEntries after capEntryCount in the enforce branch of saveSessionStoreUnlocked; add 'missing' field to SessionMaintenanceApplyReport
- src/config/sessions/store.pruning.integration.test.ts: add two reproducer tests covering fixMissing: true (prunes orphan) and fixMissing: false (preserves orphan); add fixMissing: false to ENFORCED_MAINTENANCE_OVERRIDE fixture for type completeness

Background: fleet-wide scan on 2026-05-18 found 37 orphan pointers across 10 agents in production. Manual cleanup was needed; this change makes the heal automatic for operators who opt in.

Rollout: default off for one release; flip to default on once telemetry confirms prune counts stay bounded in real installs.

Tests: 46 pass (44 existing + 2 new reproducers).

Fix scope documented at:
shared/wiki/pages/future-builds/atomic-session-prune.md

## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

Why does this matter now?

What is the intended outcome?

What is intentionally out of scope?

What does success look like?

What should reviewers focus on?

<details>
<summary>Summary guidance</summary>

This PR description is the contributor's durable explanation of the change. Write it for human maintainers first; ClawSweeper and Barnacle use the same text to understand intent, proof, risk, and current review state.

Describe the intent and outcome in 2-5 bullets. Avoid restating the diff; reviewers and bots can read the changed files.

If this PR fixes a plugin beta-release blocker, title it `fix(<plugin-id>): beta blocker - <summary>` and link the matching `Beta blocker: <plugin-name> - <summary>` issue labeled `beta-blocker`. Contributors cannot label PRs, so the title is the PR-side signal for maintainers and automation.

</details>

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

<details>
<summary>Linked context guidance</summary>

Link the issue, PR, discussion, maintainer request, or owner request that explains why this PR should exist. Maintainer context helps reviewers and automation distinguish intended work from drive-by churn.

</details>

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed:
- Real environment tested:
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
- Observed result after fix:
- What was not tested:
- Proof limitations or environment constraints:
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged):

<details>
<summary>Real behavior proof guidance</summary>

External contributors must show after-fix evidence from a real OpenClaw setup. Unit tests, mocks, lint, typechecks, snapshots, and CI are supplemental only.

Screenshots are encouraged even for CLI, console, text, or log changes. Terminal screenshots, copied live output, redacted runtime logs, recordings, and linked artifacts count.

If your environment cannot produce the ideal proof, explain that under `Proof limitations or environment constraints` so reviewers and ClawSweeper can direct the next step properly.

Be mindful of private information like IP addresses, API keys, phone numbers, non-public endpoints, or other private details when providing evidence.

</details>

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

What regression coverage was added or updated?

What failed before this fix, if known?

If no test was added, why not?

<details>
<summary>Testing guidance</summary>

List focused commands, not every incidental check. CI is useful support, but external PRs still need real behavior proof above when behavior changes.

</details>

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

What is the highest-risk area?

How is that risk mitigated?

<details>
<summary>Risk guidance</summary>

Use this for author judgment that is not obvious from the diff. ClawSweeper can see touched files, but it cannot know which behavior you think is risky, why the risk is acceptable, or what mitigation reviewers should verify.

</details>

## Current review state

What is the next action?

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

<details>
<summary>Review state guidance</summary>

Keep this as the durable state for review progress. If useful information appears in comments, fold the current next action or blocker back here so maintainers and ClawSweeper do not need to reconstruct state from comment history.

</details>

target.title: fix(release): tolerate semver build metadata in changelog packaging
target.body:
## Summary

`scripts/package-changelog.mjs` rejects valid semver build metadata, so `pnpm pack` of any package with `version: X.Y.Z+suffix` crashes during prepack with `Unsupported OpenClaw package version for changelog packaging`. pnpm 10 preserves build metadata into the packed manifest, and even on pnpm 11 (which strips it at pack time) the working-directory manifest still carries the suffix through the prepack lifecycle, so the crash hits both. Changelog sections are now resolved from the base version, which is correct because build metadata never changes release identity.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: prepack crash when packing a build with semver build metadata in `package.json`.
Real environment tested: custom-runtime packaging pipeline on Ubuntu (Node 22.22, pnpm 11.2.2), 2026-06-12.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `pnpm pack` with `version: 2026.6.6+jm`.
Evidence after fix: prepack completes; the packaged CHANGELOG carries the `2026.6.6` release section.
Observed result after fix: tarball builds and installs normally.
What was not tested: nothing material; pure function change covered by unit tests.

## Verification

- Two regression cases added to `test/scripts/package-changelog.test.ts` (`X.Y.Z+meta` and `X.Y.Z-beta.N+meta`); file passes 10/10.

target.title: fix(agents): prevent local exec policy errors from triggering model fallback
target.body:
## Summary

A Codex app-server local exec policy denial was classified as a model failure, so the agent switched providers/models on what is a local configuration outcome - burning fallback budget and changing models mid-session with no availability reason. Policy errors are now excluded from fallback classification. The change also makes an agent-level full exec policy correctly override a global allowlist for the Codex app-server.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: spurious provider/model fallback triggered by local exec policy denials.
Real environment tested: production single-operator OpenClaw gateway, 15 agents, Codex plugin active; patch live since 2026-06-12.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: trigger a denied exec in a Codex app-server lane and observe the session's model routing.
Evidence after fix: denial surfaces as a policy error; fallback chain not activated; session stays on the primary model.
Observed result after fix: policy denials are reported to the operator instead of silently degrading the model.
What was not tested: sibling runtimes with equivalent policy surfaces (non-Codex app-server paths) - flagging for reviewer judgment on whether the same classification gap exists there.

## Verification

- Tests in the same commit: `src/agents/model-fallback.test.ts` (policy errors not treated as fallback failures) and Codex app-server config tests (full exec policy overrides global allowlist).
- Running in production on a patched 2026.6.6 build since 2026-06-12.

target.title: fix(daemon): omit service environment maps from status output
target.body:
## Summary

Daemon status output included service environment maps, so any status consumer (logs, transcripts, support bundles) could capture secret-bearing values. Status now omits the environment value map and reports `environmentValueSources` instead, preserving where each value came from for debugging without the values themselves.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: secret-bearing service environment maps emitted in daemon status JSON.
Real environment tested: production single-operator OpenClaw gateway (Ubuntu, Node 22.22), patch live since 2026-06-12.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `openclaw daemon status --json` against the live gateway and node services.
Evidence after fix: no `environment` map present in status output; `environmentValueSources` present.
Observed result after fix: status/debug flows work normally with sources-only reporting.
What was not tested: third-party tooling that parsed the removed `environment` field.

## Verification

- Tests in the same commit assert env maps are absent from JSON status and value sources survive: `src/cli/daemon-cli/status.print.test.ts`, `src/cli/node-cli/daemon.test.ts`.
- Running in production on a patched 2026.6.6 build since 2026-06-12.

target.title: fix(gmail): keep gmail watch secrets out of process argv
target.body:
## Summary

The gmail watcher launched `gog watch serve` with the push token passed via `--token` and the hook token embedded in `--hook-url`, exposing both to any local process via `ps`/procfs for the lifetime of the watcher. The watcher now writes both secrets to private secret files and passes file paths on argv instead.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: gmail watch push/hook tokens visible in process listings on any multi-process host.
Real environment tested: production single-operator OpenClaw gateway (Ubuntu, Node 22.22), patch live since 2026-06-12.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `ps -ef | grep "gog watch serve"` on the live gateway with the gmail hook active.
Evidence after fix: no token material in the watcher argv; tokens resolved from secret files.
Observed result after fix: gmail watch connects and delivers push notifications normally.
What was not tested: Windows/macOS service-manager spawn paths.

## Verification

- Unit tests added in the same commit assert built args carry file paths and never raw tokens: `src/hooks/gmail-watcher.test.ts`, `src/hooks/gmail.test.ts`.
- Running in production on a patched 2026.6.6 build since 2026-06-12.

target.title: fix(memory-core): preserve keyword-only hybrid results
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes hybrid memory search so keyword-only FTS hits survive the post-merge minScore boundary even when another vector result already passes the strict threshold.

Fixes #92337.

## Real Behavior Proof

- Behavior addressed: Hybrid search no longer drops valid keyword-only FTS hits just because at least one vector result is already strict.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Users/stellarfish/openclaw-92337-boundary`, running the memory-core manager through `node_modules/.bin/tsx --conditions=development` with a real sqlite memory index, trigram FTS enabled, and a local mock embedding provider registered through OpenClaw's provider registry.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Created an isolated workspace with `memory/vector.md` and `memory/keyword.md`, configured `store.fts.tokenizer: "trigram"`, synced through `getMemorySearchManager(...).sync()`, then ran `manager.search("飞书", { maxResults: 10, minScore: 0.35 })`.
- Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "query": "飞书",
  "fts": {
    "enabled": true,
    "available": true
  },
  "resultCount": 2,
  "results": [
    {
      "path": "memory/vector.md",
      "score": 0.7,
      "vectorScore": 1,
      "textScore": 0,
      "snippet": "# Vector\nvector-anchor semantic entry\n"
    },
    {
      "path": "memory/keyword.md",
      "score": 0.3,
      "vectorScore": 0,
      "textScore": 1,
      "snippet": "# Keyword\n飞书插件配置 已完成\n"
    }
  ]
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The strict vector result remains first, and the keyword-only trigram FTS result is also preserved with `vectorScore: 0`, `textScore: 1`, and weighted score `0.3`, even though `minScore` is `0.35`.
- What was not tested: Live user Ollama embeddings; the proof uses OpenClaw's real manager/sqlite/FTS path with a deterministic local embedding provider to avoid external credentials.

## Changes

- Move the preservation behavior to the manager search filtering boundary instead of inferring chunk identity from overlapping line ranges.
- Keep `mergeHybridResults` exact-id semantics and leave the shared FTS token normalizer untouched.
- Preserve current main's relaxed keyword-backed fallback when all weighted hybrid scores are below `minScore`, including overlapping vector+keyword hits.
- Preserve keyword-only FTS hits when strict results exist while keeping the order already returned by `mergeHybridResults`, including MMR/temporal ordering.
- Add regression coverage for keyword-only hybrid hits, overlapping keyword-backed fallback, and merge-order preservation.

## Validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts -t "preserves keyword-only hybrid hits|overlapping keyword-backed|hybrid merger order"` — 4 passed, 47 skipped
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/hybrid.test.ts` — 5 passed
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo`
- `git diff --check -- extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts`

## Risks

- The result window is still capped by `maxResults`, so keyword-only hits can be trimmed if higher-ranked selected results fill the requested limit.

target.title: fix(agents): preserve runtime tool inheritance tokens
target.body:
## Summary
- Preserve runtime-materialized allowlist tokens when spawned sessions inherit an effective parent tool surface.
- Filter deferred MCP/LSP selectors through the same effective profile/agent/group/subagent/inherited policy layers before writing child inherited allowlists, so broad `bundle-mcp`/`group:plugins` entries cannot bypass narrower policies.
- Cover the shared helper, gateway resolution, and embedded tool construction paths, including broad-allow then narrow-allow/deny regressions.

Refs #85030

## Verification
- `corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/agents/tool-policy.ts src/gateway/tool-resolution.ts src/agents/agent-tools.ts src/agents/tool-policy.test.ts src/gateway/tool-resolution.exclude.test.ts src/agents/agent-tools.create-openclaw-coding-tools.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tool-policy.test.ts src/gateway/tool-resolution.exclude.test.ts src/agents/agent-tools.create-openclaw-coding-tools.test.ts`
- `corepack pnpm test:docker:cron-mcp-cleanup`
- Temporary local proof test, removed after capture: `corepack pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/openclaw-sessions-spawn-mcp-proof.temp.test.ts --reporter verbose`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Spawned subagent inherited allowlists dropped runtime materialization policy tokens such as `bundle-mcp`, `group:plugins`, MCP `server__tool` names, and `lsp_` tool names when the parent effective tool surface did not already contain materialized runtime tools; preserving those tokens must also respect narrower effective policy layers.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, Docker 29.4.0, repository Docker E2E image built from this branch, real Gateway process, mock OpenAI-compatible server, and real stdio MCP probe servers. The focused spawned-child MCP proof used the real `sessions_spawn` tool executor, the production subagent spawn executor, production child session patching, production `getOrCreateSessionMcpRuntime`/`materializeBundleMcpToolsForRun`, child attempt allowlist filtering, and final effective tool policy.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: After commit `419086e4c141d03874d7bec1730c099a96a84b55`, ran `corepack pnpm test:docker:cron-mcp-cleanup`. Then ran a temporary focused proof test with:

```text
corepack pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/openclaw-sessions-spawn-mcp-proof.temp.test.ts --reporter verbose
```

That proof created a temporary stdio MCP server with two tools, `allowed` and `blocked`; configured `sessions_spawn` with the narrowed inherited allowlist `["read", "sessions_spawn", "probe__allowed"]`; executed a real `sessions_spawn` call; captured the child session patch written by the spawn executor; wrote the captured child store to the configured session store path; materialized both MCP tools for the spawned child session; then ran the same child attempt allowlist/final policy filtering and invoked the surviving MCP tool.

Evidence after fix: Focused spawned-child terminal output with UUIDs redacted:

```json
{
  "sessionsSpawnInheritedAllowInput": [
    "read",
    "sessions_spawn",
    "probe__allowed"
  ],
  "spawnResult": {
    "status": "accepted",
    "childSessionKey": "agent:main:subagent:<uuid>",
    "runId": "proof-run-1",
    "mode": "run",
    "modelApplied": true
  },
  "childSessionKey": "agent:main:subagent:<uuid>",
  "childInheritedAllow": [
    "read",
    "sessions_spawn",
    "probe__allowed"
  ],
  "capturedStoreEntry": {
    "spawnDepth": 1,
    "subagentRole": "orchestrator",
    "subagentControlScope": "children",
    "inheritedToolAllow": [
      "read",
      "sessions_spawn",
      "probe__allowed"
    ],
    "spawnedBy": "agent:main:main"
  },
  "gatewayAgentCallSeen": true,
  "materializedToolNames": [
    "probe__allowed",
    "probe__blocked"
  ],
  "childVisibleMcpToolNames": [
    "probe__allowed"
  ],
  "blockedVisible": false,
  "invocationResult": {
    "content": [
      {
        "type": "text",
        "text": "subagent-sessions-spawn-mcp-allowed-ok"
      }
    ],
    "details": {
      "mcpServer": "probe",
      "mcpTool": "allowed"
    }
  }
}
```

The temporary proof passed in both Vitest projects that selected it:

```text
✓ |agents-core| proof: real sessions_spawn child receives and enforces narrowed MCP inherited allowlist
✓ |agents-support| proof: real sessions_spawn child receives and enforces narrowed MCP inherited allowlist

Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests       2 passed (2)
```

Additional real Docker output from `corepack pnpm test:docker:cron-mcp-cleanup`:

```text
OpenClaw package tarball integrity passed.
Running in-container cron/subagent MCP cleanup smoke...
{"ok":true,"cron":{"jobId":"ed353e39-08bc-408e-8867-6a99df36a679","runId":"manual:ed353e39-08bc-408e-8867-6a99df36a679:1781294149608:1","pid":282,"status":"ok"},"subagent":{"runId":"d6a7af12-ed53-416a-b917-8f0c234d2f14","exitedPids":[295],"pids":[295]}}
OK
```

Observed result after fix: The real `sessions_spawn` path accepted a child run, wrote `inheritedToolAllow: ["read", "sessions_spawn", "probe__allowed"]` onto the spawned child session, the child materialized both MCP tools from the stdio server, final child-visible tools included only `probe__allowed`, the conflicting `probe__blocked` tool was absent, and the allowed MCP tool executed successfully.

What was not tested: A credentialed external MCP server or a live paid LLM provider. The proof uses local stdio MCP servers and the repository mock/test runtime for deterministic behavior.

target.title: Suppress failed tool progress in Discord
target.body:
## Summary

- skip failed/error item progress before Discord draft rendering
- skip failed command-output progress before Discord draft rendering
- preserve existing fallback tool-error warning delivery for unacknowledged failures
- add Discord monitor regressions for failed item progress and failed command output

## Verification

- `pnpm test extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.test.ts` passes: 106 tests
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Discord can leak internal failed tool progress as channel-visible draft text after an otherwise normal run.

Real environment tested: Local Vitest regression coverage in a fresh fork checkout; separately observed a locally patched installed Discord canary suppressing the failed progress leak before preparing this PR.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `pnpm test extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.test.ts`.

Evidence after fix: The Discord monitor test file passes all 106 tests, including new assertions that failed item progress and failed command output do not update Discord draft text.

Observed result after fix: Failed/error tool progress remains out of Discord-visible draft output while existing fallback tool-error warning behavior is preserved by leaving `suppressToolErrorWarnings` unset.

What was not tested: No live Discord bot run was executed from this source checkout, and broader extension/full CI was not run locally.

target.title: [codex] Fail closed pair slash command routing
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #98239.

Running `/pair qr` from Control UI webchat could leave an externally managed Tailscale Serve Control UI route broken. The reported production route had Tailscale Serve proxying the HTTPS webchat URL to `http://127.0.0.1:18789` while OpenClaw later ended up with `gateway.bind=tailnet`, so the Gateway no longer served the loopback backend and the webchat URL returned HTTP 502.

The direct device-pair QR generator is non-mutating, but the reported production behavior showed an interrupted webchat task re-applying a `gateway.bind=tailnet` change after manual loopback restore. The likely writer is a missed plugin command falling through to a normal agent turn, especially with `/ pair qr` syntax or an unavailable command runtime. Once the agent turn owns the request, it can attempt configuration repair and restart the Gateway.

## Why This Change Was Made

This PR keeps device pairing URL generation and slash dispatch from silently widening `gateway.bind` or falling into a general agent turn:

- accepts `/ pair qr` as the registered `/pair` plugin command, so whitespace after the slash does not bypass the plugin handler
- reserves manifest-declared runtime slash commands when no runtime handler is registered, returning a deterministic plugin-unavailable response instead of continuing into the agent pipeline
- filters manifest runtime slash reservations through plugin activation policy, so disabled or denied plugins do not reserve commands
- keeps built-in/reserved aliases out of manifest-only reservations
- gates manifest fallback responses through the normal command authorization check
- updates loopback-only pairing guidance to keep `gateway.bind=loopback` for externally managed Tailscale Serve setups and use `gateway.tailscale.mode=serve`, `plugins.entries.device-pair.config.publicUrl`, or `gateway.remote.url` for the public mobile pairing URL
- adds explicit model-facing gateway tool guard coverage for `gateway.bind` changes via `config.patch` and `config.apply`

## User Impact

For users opening Control UI through Tailscale Serve, `/pair qr` should no longer be able to miss the plugin command and continue as a normal agent request that may mutate Gateway bind settings. If the device-pair runtime handler is not registered yet, users get a deterministic plugin-unavailable response instead of a hidden agent turn.

Externally managed Tailscale Serve setups also get clearer loopback guidance: keep the Gateway loopback bind stable and configure the public pairing URL through managed Serve mode, plugin `publicUrl`, or `gateway.remote.url`.

## Evidence

Focused local validation:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/command-control.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-plugin.test.ts src/plugins/commands.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/commands.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-plugin.test.ts extensions/device-pair/index.test.ts src/pairing/setup-code.test.ts src/agents/tools/gateway-tool-guard-coverage.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts test/scripts/plugin-sdk-surface-report.test.ts --reporter=verbose`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `pnpm tsgo:core:test`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test`
- `pnpm check:import-cycles`
- `pnpm check:no-conflict-markers`
- `pnpm lint:auth:no-pairing-store-group`
- `pnpm lint:auth:pairing-account-scope`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/auto-reply/command-detection.ts src/auto-reply/command-control.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-plugin.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-plugin.test.ts src/plugins/commands.ts src/plugins/commands.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/device-pair/index.test.ts extensions/device-pair/index.ts`
- `git diff --check`

Review validation:

- `codex review --base origin/main` on head `434a7f18`: no actionable correctness issues found.
- Local ClawSweeper review on head `434a7f18`: no code findings and no security findings; remaining blocker was real behavior proof, now added below.

Live after-fix behavior proof on the production VPS, with prod restored after proof:

- Backed up active config, Tailscale Serve status, and the installed global OpenClaw package.
- Temporarily installed this PR package: `OpenClaw 2026.6.11 (434a7f1)`.
- Recreated the reported precondition deliberately: `gateway.bind=loopback`; Tailscale Serve `/` -> `http://127.0.0.1:18789`; local health `200`; HTTPS Control UI root `200`; HTTPS `/healthz` `200`.
- Used a temporary random gateway token for the proof window only; QR/setup contents and token were not printed in PR evidence.
- Sent the Control UI/WebChat `chat.send` RPC for `/pair qr`: ACK `{"ok":true,"status":"started","runIdPresent":true}`.
- Sent the Control UI/WebChat `chat.send` RPC for `/ pair qr`: ACK `{"ok":true,"status":"started","runIdPresent":true}`.
- Waited 30 seconds after the sends.
- After the wait: `gateway.bind=loopback`; Tailscale Serve `/` still -> `http://127.0.0.1:18789`; HTTPS Control UI root `200`; HTTPS `/healthz` `200`.
- Restored production state: `OpenClaw 2026.6.10 (aa69b12)`; `gateway.bind=tailnet`; Tailscale Serve `/` -> `http://[redacted-tailnet-ip]:18789`; HTTPS Control UI root `200`; HTTPS `/healthz` `200`.

Redacted proof summary from VPS artifact `~/.openclaw/proof-98262-20260630T230842Z/proof-summary.txt`:

```text
before_version=OpenClaw 2026.6.10 (aa69b12)
pr_version=OpenClaw 2026.6.11 (434a7f1)
pre_bind=loopback
pre_serve_root=http://127.0.0.1:18789
pair_qr_ack={"ok":true,"status":"started","runIdPresent":true}
spaced_pair_qr_ack={"ok":true,"status":"started","runIdPresent":true}
after_bind=loopback
after_serve_root=http://127.0.0.1:18789
after_https_root=200
after_https_health=200
restore_bind=tailnet
restore_serve_root=http://[redacted-tailnet-ip]:18789
restore_https_root=200
restore_https_health=200
```

`pnpm check:changed` was attempted first. On this Windows host it delegated to Blacksmith Testbox and failed before running checks because the local Crabbox binary reports `main-a2c78e86` instead of a parseable semver. Running the local child wrapper also hung without output, so the relevant changed-check typecheck/lint/guard commands above were run directly.

target.title: fix: improve NO_PROXY handling to support .myqcloud.com for COS uploads
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: EnvHttpProxyAgent's NO_PROXY matching does not support leading-dot wildcard patterns (e.g., `.myqcloud.com`). This causes all COS (Tencent Cloud Object Storage) requests to incorrectly route through the proxy, breaking qqbot plugin's chunked upload when HTTPS_PROXY is set.
- Why it matters: Users with proxy configurations cannot send images via qqbot plugin; the feature completely fails with "fetch failed" errors.
- What changed: Enhanced `ensureGlobalUndiciStreamTimeouts()` to expand NO_PROXY entries using `expandNoProxyPatterns()`, automatically including common COS domains (`myqcloud.com`, `.myqcloud.com`) and supporting leading-dot suffix matching.
- What did NOT change: The overall undici dispatcher architecture, timeout handling, and autoSelectFamily logic remain unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #95998

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- COS uploads via qqbot plugin now work correctly when HTTPS_PROXY/HTTP_PROXY environment variables are set and NO_PROXY includes (or automatically includes) `.myqcloud.com`.
- No visible changes for users without proxy configurations.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`) - only routing logic improved
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: (none)

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS Darwin 25.5.0 (arm64)
- Runtime/container: Node.js v22.22.1
- Integration/channel: qqbot plugin (@tencent-connect/openclaw-qqbot v1.7.2)
- Relevant config: HTTPS_PROXY set, NO_PROXY may or may not include .myqcloud.com

### Steps

1. Set HTTPS_PROXY to a proxy (e.g., `export HTTPS_PROXY=http://127.0.0.1:7890`)
2. Start OpenClaw gateway
3. Send an image via qqbot using `<qqmedia>` tag
4. Observe COS chunked upload

### Expected

COS PUT request succeeds, image delivered to target chat.

### Actual (before fix)

"fetch failed" error after ~1 second timeout; image not delivered.

## Evidence

- Added unit tests for `expandNoProxyPatterns` function covering leading-dot expansion, auto-inclusion of COS domains, preservation of existing entries, empty input handling, and deduplication.
- All existing tests in `undici-global-dispatcher.test.ts` continue to pass (no behavior change for non-proxy or standard NO_PROXY cases).

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Reviewed the TypeScript implementation for correct string manipulation and array handling.
- Ran standalone Node.js script to verify `expandNoProxyPatterns` logic produces expected output:
  - `.example.com` → includes both `".example.com"` and `"example.com"`
  - `"localhost"` → automatically includes `"myqcloud.com"` and `".myqcloud.com"`
  - Existing entries preserved and deduplicated correctly.
- Confirmed that `EnvHttpProxyAgent` accepts `noProxy` option and will use the expanded array for host matching.
- Verified that no other dispatcher kinds (Agent, ProxyAgent) are affected.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`) - Existing NO_PROXY configurations continue to work; additional entries are only added.
- Config/env changes? (`No`) - The fix is automatic; no user configuration required.
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: (not applicable)

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert this commit; the change is limited to two files in `src/infra/net/`.
- Files/config to restore: `src/infra/net/undici-global-dispatcher.ts` and `src/infra/net/undici-global-dispatcher.test.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: None expected; the change is additive and isolated.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: The expanded noProxy list could inadvertently match hosts that should go through proxy.
  - Mitigation: Only auto-adds well-known COS domains (`myqcloud.com`) that are known to need direct connection; these are specific to Tencent Cloud storage used by qqbot plugin.
- Risk: Edge cases in domain matching logic could produce false negatives/positives.
  - Mitigation: Comprehensive unit tests cover common patterns; the logic is straightforward string suffix matching.

commands.author: Solvely-Colin

target.title: fix: unify Android Voice setup readiness
target.body:
Closes #98268

AI-assisted: yes. Sanitized transcript logs were not included.

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where Android users opening Voice, Realtime Talk, Dictation, or Voice Setup could see inconsistent configured/unconfigured state when Gateway Talk catalog readiness differed between realtime voice and realtime transcription. It also resolves a problem where Talk/Dictation startup failures could leave only clipped status text instead of durable setup guidance or readable provider details.

## Why This Change Was Made

Android now derives Realtime Talk and Dictation readiness from the Gateway `talk.catalog` / `talk.config` contract through one shared readiness model used by both the Voice tab and Voice Setup. Startup/provider failures stay on Voice with a persistent issue panel, setup action, and sanitized expandable details, without changing Gateway/provider implementation or unrelated Android onboarding/settings flows.

The readiness gate treats confirmed catalog setup failures differently from unknown catalog state: confirmed unconfigured or empty provider rows route to Voice Setup, while catalog-load failures or unmatched active provider aliases leave the Gateway session API authoritative instead of blocking Talk/Dictation locally.

## User Impact

Users now see the same nouns and configured truth on Voice and Voice Setup: Realtime Talk can need setup while Dictation is ready, and both surfaces say so consistently. When provider startup fails, users stay in the Voice flow and can open setup or expand sanitized details instead of getting bounced back with a clipped one-line error.

## Evidence

- Focused Android unit tests passed after the latest review fixes:

```shell
JAVA_HOME='/Applications/Android Studio.app/Contents/jbr/Contents/Home' ANDROID_HOME="$HOME/Library/Android/sdk" ANDROID_SDK_ROOT="$HOME/Library/Android/sdk" ./gradlew --no-daemon :app:testPlayDebugUnitTest --tests ai.openclaw.app.GatewayTalkSetupReadinessTest --tests ai.openclaw.app.ui.VoiceScreenLogicTest --tests ai.openclaw.app.voice.TalkModeManagerTest
# BUILD SUCCESSFUL in 16s
# 30 actionable tasks: 5 executed, 25 up-to-date
```

- Whitespace check passed:

```shell
git diff --check
```

- Autoreview passed clean against refreshed `origin/main`:

```shell
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main
# autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported
# overall: patch is correct (0.86)
```

- Local emulator proof captured on `OpenClaw_FinalProof_API_36` with the Play debug app installed. Visual artifacts were kept outside the repository per PR artifact policy, scanned for obvious secret-shaped strings, and not committed.

Voice tab readiness and persistent setup issue panel:

![Android Voice readiness showing Realtime Talk needs setup and Dictation ready](https://files.catbox.moe/3qsm30.png)

Voice Setup using the same Gateway catalog-derived rows:

![Android Voice Setup showing Realtime Talk needs setup and Dictation ready](https://files.catbox.moe/803w5i.png)

Voice to Voice Setup flow proof:

![Android Voice setup flow GIF](https://files.catbox.moe/17o7k5.gif)

commands.author: totobusnello

target.title: fix(agents): preserve streamed assistant text when Claude CLI result event is empty
target.body:
## Summary

- The claude `stream-json` dialect can emit a final `result` event with **empty text** even though assistant text deltas were already streamed (or the turn was tool-only). Both JSONL parsers trust the final event and discard everything accumulated, so the CLI runner throws `CLI backend returned an empty response.` (`reason=empty_response`) and the gateway falls back to the next model — **losing a reply that had already fully arrived**.
- Fix (parser-only): when the `result` event has no text, fall back to the accumulated streamed deltas instead of dropping them, in **both** `createCliJsonlStreamingParser` and `parseCliJsonl`. A structured `CliOutput.hadToolCalls` flag (set via `claudeRecordHasToolUseBlock` / the existing `isClaudeToolUseBlockType`) is also recorded so a verifiably tool-only turn can be told apart from a genuinely empty reply.
- **The runner's empty-response failover contract is unchanged.** An earlier revision of this PR added a `hadToolCalls`-based bypass in `cli-runner.ts`; that was removed (commit `ac2f807c`, following Option 1 of the review). Today an empty turn — tool-only or not — still fails over by default, and only stays silent when the caller already set `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent`. **This PR no longer touches `cli-runner.ts`** (it is byte-identical to `main`); the only behavioral change lives in the two parsers.
- The parser already half-acknowledges this case — `parseClaudeCliJsonlResult` deliberately keeps session metadata when the result text is empty (*"Keep the resolved session handle and usage instead of dropping them"*). This PR extends the same reasoning to the streamed assistant text itself.
- Safety: if the model genuinely returned nothing, the accumulated text is also empty, so the `FailoverError` fires exactly as before. Non-Claude dialects are untouched (`hadToolCalls` stays `undefined`).
- Reviewers should focus on: the fallback expression in **both** parsers and the structured tool_use detection helper (`claudeRecordHasToolUseBlock`). `cli-runner.ts` is **not** part of this diff.
- The vulnerable path (`if (result) { output = result; return; }`) is still present on `main` and in the published `2026.6.8` tarball (`dist/claude-live-session-*.js` / `dist/cli-output-*.js`); see the 2026-06-16 comment confirming it is not fixed natively in 6.7/6.8.

## Tests

- `cli-output.test.ts`: 4 new cases — streamed text preserved on empty result (both parsers), tool-only flag set (both parsers); updated the existing "preserves Claude session metadata even when the final result text is empty" expectation (`hadToolCalls: false`).
- `cli-runner.reliability.test.ts`: focused coverage that a verifiably tool-only empty turn (tool_use block + empty result) **fails over by default** (`empty_response`) and only returns the silent payload when `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent` is set — i.e. the prior fallback contract is preserved.
- `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test` run locally (macOS): the only failures are environment-specific (host locale leaking into `Intl` formatting asserts and `/tmp` → `/private/tmp` symlink canonicalization), none in the touched files.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Claude CLI turns streamed a full reply, but the final `result` event arrived with empty text; the gateway threw `CLI backend returned an empty response.` (`reason=empty_response`) and fell back to `openai/gpt-5.5`, discarding the streamed reply. Tool-only turns hit the same false failover.
- Real environment tested: production OpenClaw **2026.6.1** gateway on an Ubuntu VPS (systemd service), `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6` and `claude-haiku-4-5` via the claude-cli backend, fallback chain to `openai/gpt-5.5`, live Discord/WhatsApp channels and cron agents. Still reproducing through **2026.6.8** (see the 2026-06-16 comment).
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: applied the equivalent fix as a dist-bundle patch (same logic as this PR's source change) to `dist/claude-live-session-*.js` and `dist/cli-output-*.js`, validated with `node --check`, then `systemctl restart openclaw-gateway` (2026-06-04 13:56 BRT) and watched `journalctl -u openclaw-gateway -f` under normal production traffic; later verified with `ssh` to the host and `journalctl -u openclaw-gateway --since "14:26" | grep -c empty_response`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): redacted runtime logs / copied live output below. After the restart: `journalctl -u openclaw-gateway --since "14:26" | grep -c empty_response` → `0` (vs 3 occurrences earlier that same day), and the gateway boot shows claude sessions healthy:

  ```
  Jun 04 14:26:26 [gateway] http server listening (11 plugins: anthropic, codex, discord, ...)
  Jun 04 14:26:27 [gateway] ready
  Jun 04 14:26:27 [heartbeat] started
  Jun 04 14:26:29 [whatsapp] Listening for WhatsApp inbound messages (DM + 2 configured groups).
  ```

- Observed result after fix: zero `empty_response` fallbacks across several hours of live traffic since the restart; claude turns that previously produced `outBytes=0` now deliver the streamed text and stay on the requested model. The tool-only half of this fix has been running in the same production gateway since 2026-05-31, the empty-response half since 2026-06-04 — zero false `empty_response` fallbacks across every release since (6.5 / 6.6 / 6.8).
- What was not tested: non-Claude CLI backends (gemini/codex dialects — code paths untouched, `hadToolCalls` stays `undefined` for them); `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent=true` beyond existing unit coverage.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the empty-result event is intermittent (3 occurrences on 2026-06-04 before the patch, none reproducible on demand); an instrumented hook in the production gateway dumps the raw JSONL stream on the next occurrence, and we will attach a sanitized capture as a follow-up comment.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): three occurrences on 2026-06-04 before the patch. Note `outHash=e3b0c44298fc` — the SHA-256 of the empty string — after 40/66/86 streamed JSONL lines: a full stream arrived, exactly zero bytes were delivered, and the turn was answered by the fallback model:

  ```
  Jun 04 09:56:23 [agent/cli-backend] claude live session turn: provider=claude-cli model=claude-sonnet-4-6 durationMs=12765 rawLines=40 outBytes=0 outHash=e3b0c44298fc
  Jun 04 09:56:23 [model-fallback/decision] decision=candidate_failed requested=anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6 candidate=anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6 reason=empty_response next=openai/gpt-5.5 detail=CLI backend returned an empty response.
  Jun 04 13:42:52 [agent/cli-backend] claude live session turn: provider=claude-cli model=claude-sonnet-4-6 durationMs=30337 rawLines=66 outBytes=0 outHash=e3b0c44298fc
  Jun 04 13:42:52 [model-fallback/decision] decision=candidate_failed requested=anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6 candidate=anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6 reason=empty_response next=openai/gpt-5.5 detail=CLI backend returned an empty response.
  Jun 04 13:45:51 [agent/cli-backend] claude live session turn: provider=claude-cli model=claude-sonnet-4-6 durationMs=36624 rawLines=86 outBytes=0 outHash=e3b0c44298fc
  Jun 04 13:45:51 [model-fallback/decision] decision=candidate_failed requested=anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6 candidate=anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6 reason=empty_response next=openai/gpt-5.5 detail=CLI backend returned an empty response.
  ```

target.title: fix: show in-progress status for channel runs
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users sending messages through a channel such as Weixin would not see the Control UI run status switch to "In progress" while the agent loop was running.

WebChat-started runs already showed the active state, but channel-started reply runs could leave the session row looking idle in Control UI.

## Why This Change Was Made

This routes every gateway session payload that projects `hasActiveRun` through a single visible active-run helper. The helper keeps the existing Control UI chat abort-controller behavior and also checks the embedded/channel reply-run registry by `sessionId`, so list, startup/history, session-change, and transcript-message snapshots all agree.

## User Impact

Users can now watch channel-triggered agent work in Control UI and see the same "In progress" status they already get for WebChat-triggered runs.

## Evidence

- Focused tests:
  - `pnpm test src/gateway/server-session-events.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/sessions.abort-agent-scope.test.ts`
  - Result: 4 files passed, 50 tests passed.
- Formatting/diff guard:
  - `git diff --check HEAD`
  - Result: passed.
- Local ClawSweeper review:
  - `pnpm run review -- --local-only --local-range --target-repo openclaw/openclaw --target-dir ../openclaw --base origin/main --codex-timeout-ms 600000`
  - Result: `review_status: complete`, `Overall correctness: patch is correct`, `Full review comments: none`.

target.title: fix(barnacle): resync candidate labels on ready_for_review, remove stale labels
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Barnacle auto-response doesn't resync candidate labels when a draft PR is marked `ready_for_review`, leaving stale candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) on PRs with meaningful bodies.
- Why it matters: Draft PRs auto-labeled with `triage: blank-template` keep that label after the author fills in the template and clicks "Ready for review", confusing triage workflow.
- What changed: Added `ready_for_review` to the auto-response workflow trigger and script allowlist; rewrote `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` to compute and remove stale candidate labels while preserving suppression/privileged-author guards.
- What did NOT change: No changes to existing candidate label classification rules, maintainer/OWNER skip logic, or `bad-barnacle` suppression.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #77343
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Three gaps — (1) `.github/workflows/auto-response.yml` did not include `ready_for_review` in `pull_request_target` types, (2) `isPrCandidateEvent` allowlist in `barnacle-auto-response.mjs` did not include `ready_for_review`, and (3) `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` was add-only (never removed stale labels).
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test for the `ready_for_review` → stale label removal path.
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Real behavior proof

Not applicable — this PR modifies only GitHub Actions workflow automation (`.github/workflows/auto-response.yml`).
No product code, CLI, gateway, plugin, or UI surface is touched.
Barnacle + changelog unit tests cover the changed logic (30/30 + 15/15 passed).

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `test/scripts/barnacle-auto-response.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `ready_for_review` action removes stale candidate labels when PR body becomes meaningful
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Directly exercises `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` return value + `removeLabels` call path
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None — this was a new coverage gap
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

When a maintainer-owned PR transitions from draft to `ready_for_review` (or is edited), Barnacle recomputes candidate labels and removes any stale Barnacle-owned candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) that no longer match the PR body/files.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [add missing labels only] -> [stale labels persist]

After:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [recompute labels] -> [add missing + remove stale] -> [labels match body]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No (existing GitHub API calls)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any (GitHub Actions)
- Runtime/container: Node 24 (GitHub Actions)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: GitHub auto-response workflow

### Steps

1. Open a draft PR with a blank issue template body
2. Barnacle auto-labels it with `triage: blank-template`
3. Edit body to fill in the template (Problem/Why/What)
4. Click "Ready for review"

### Expected

- `triage: blank-template` is removed; correct candidate labels applied

### Actual

- `triage: blank-template` persisted (before fix)

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `ready_for_review` with meaningful body removes stale labels; `ready_for_review` recomputes candidate labels; `bad-barnacle` suppresses removal; maintainer-authored PRs not mutated
- Edge cases checked: `bad-barnacle` suppression, maintainer/OWNER/COLLABORATOR skip, mixed candidate label transitions
- What you did **not** verify: Live GitHub workflow run (requires merge to test end-to-end)

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Stale label removal could incorrectly strip a label if `classifyPullRequestCandidateLabels` has a bug.
  - Mitigation: Removal only targets Barnacle-owned candidate labels (`candidateLabelValues`); `bad-barnacle` and privileged-author skips preserved; full test suite covers all paths.

target.title: test(plugins): cover filtered bundle MCP servers
target.body:
## Summary

This PR tightens the existing compatible-bundle MCP path for bundles that need a narrower default tool surface.

- Adds regression coverage that a Codex-compatible bundle `.mcp.json` preserves `toolFilter.include`, `toolFilter.exclude`, and a relative bundle data root.
- Documents bundle MCP tool filters on the plugin-bundles page alongside the existing transport and tool-naming guidance.
- Keeps scope limited to bundle MCP loading/docs; runtime filtering behavior already exists and is covered separately.

Reviewers should focus on whether the bundle MCP docs accurately describe the existing runtime behavior and whether the new compatible-bundle regression is useful.

## Linked context

No issue filed.

Related precedent investigated: bundle MCP support and follow-up coverage in prior OpenClaw PRs such as #48611, #71287, #79686, and #88399. This PR follows that existing bundle MCP seam.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

Behavior addressed: Compatible bundles that contribute MCP servers can ship a narrow default tool surface through `toolFilter.include` and `toolFilter.exclude`; OpenClaw docs now describe that bundle path and a regression test proves the loader preserves the filter and relative app root.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `test/filtered-bundle-mcp`, commit `d5bffb3dc0`, with an isolated temporary `HOME`, `OPENCLAW_HOME`, and `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Created a temporary Codex-compatible bundle containing `.codex-plugin/plugin.json`, `.mcp.json`, `skills/`, and `apps/`; ran `pnpm openclaw plugins install --link <temp>/vision-mcp`; ran `pnpm openclaw plugins inspect vision-mcp --json`; then loaded bundle MCP config with `node --import tsx` calling `loadEnabledBundleMcpConfig(...)` against the isolated home/config.

Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the temporary real OpenClaw setup:

```text
Proof root: /var/folders/.../openclaw-bundle-proof.gE7Fjt
$ node scripts/run-node.mjs plugins install --link /var/folders/.../openclaw-bundle-proof.gE7Fjt/vision-mcp
Linked plugin path: /var/folders/.../openclaw-bundle-proof.gE7Fjt/vision-mcp
Restart the gateway to load plugins.
$ node scripts/run-node.mjs plugins inspect vision-mcp --json
{
  "diagnostics": [],
  "command": "npx",
  "args": [
    "-y",
    "@vision-mcp/cli@latest",
    "serve",
    "--apps-root",
    "/private/var/folders/.../openclaw-bundle-proof.gE7Fjt/vision-mcp/apps"
  ],
  "cwdEndsWith": true,
  "toolFilter": {
    "include": [
      "capsule.*",
      "vision_map.snapshot",
      "vision_map.list_apps",
      "vision_map.list_workflows",
      "vision_map.describe_workflow",
      "vision_map.describe",
      "vision_map.detect_state",
      "vision_map.list_actions",
      "vision_map.describe_action",
      "vision_map.perform_action",
      "vision_map.run_workflow",
      "vision_map.verify",
      "vision_map.repair_minimal",
      "vision_map.export_trace"
    ],
    "exclude": [
      "vision_map.click_at",
      "vision_map.type_text",
      "vision_map.press_key",
      "vision_map.scroll",
      "vision_map.init",
      "vision_map.apply_patch",
      "vision_map.add_control",
      "vision_map.commit_*",
      "vision_map.harvest_session"
    ]
  }
}
```

Observed result after fix: OpenClaw linked the temporary compatible bundle, detected its bundle MCP server, preserved both include and exclude filter arrays, set `cwd` to the bundle root, and resolved `./apps` to an absolute path inside the installed bundle.

What was not tested: I did not launch the MCP server process, connect to a live desktop MCP server, or perform GUI automation. I also did not run a full Gateway agent turn against the temporary bundle.

Proof limitations or environment constraints: The proof uses a temporary local compatible bundle and the real OpenClaw plugin installer/loader, but the MCP server command itself is not started because this PR changes documentation and loader regression coverage, not MCP runtime execution behavior.

## Tests and validation

Commands run:

```text
node scripts/test-projects.mjs -- src/plugins/bundle-mcp.test.ts --reporter=verbose
pnpm format:check src/plugins/bundle-mcp.test.ts
git diff --check
pnpm check:docs
codex review --base origin/main
```

Regression coverage added: `src/plugins/bundle-mcp.test.ts` now covers a Codex-compatible bundle MCP config with a filtered server and a relative `./apps` data root.

Validation results:

```text
src/plugins/bundle-mcp.test.ts: 1 file passed, 8 tests passed
pnpm format:check src/plugins/bundle-mcp.test.ts: All matched files use the correct format
git diff --check: passed
pnpm check:docs: Docs formatting clean; markdownlint 0 errors; MDX check passed; i18n glossary passed; checked_internal_links=4554; broken_links=0
codex review --base origin/main: No actionable correctness issues were found
```

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`): No runtime behavior change; docs now describe existing bundle MCP filter behavior.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`): No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`): No runtime change. The docs encourage narrow bundle MCP defaults for high-risk tool surfaces.

What is the highest-risk area? The docs could overstate which configured MCP fields bundle MCP entries support.

How is that risk mitigated? Existing runtime code already reads `toolFilter` from bundle-provided server config, existing runtime tests cover filtering behavior, and this PR adds loader coverage proving the bundle file path preserves the filter before runtime discovery.

## Current review state

AI-assisted: yes.

Next action: maintainer review.

target.title: fix(mattermost): bound null-body error response reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`readMattermostError()` had a `!res.body` fallback that bypassed the bounded reader and called native `res.json()` / `res.text()` directly. For a null-body error response with a JSON content-type (a 401/503 with `content-type: application/json` and an empty body — common from reverse proxies and gateways), `await res.json()` throws `SyntaxError: Unexpected end of JSON input`. That rejection propagates out of `readMattermostError()`, so the caller's intended `Mattermost API <status>: unknown error` is replaced by a confusing JSON-parse crash. It was also the last remaining unbounded `json()` / `text()` read in this error path after #96033 bounded the success path.

Affected surface: `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/client.ts` — `readMattermostError()`, reached by `client.request()`, the file-upload path, and `probe.ts`.

## Why This Change Was Made

The `!res.body` special-case is removed entirely, so every error read goes through the existing bounded `readResponseTextLimited` (8 KiB) and the JSON `message` is parsed from that bounded text. `readResponseTextLimited` returns `""` when there is no readable body, so a null-body response now yields an empty detail instead of throwing — and all three callers already coalesce that to a clean message (`detail || "unknown error"` / `detail || res.statusText`). One canonical read path, no second buffering strategy.

Net change is a deletion (`client.ts` +0/−10): removes the divergent branch rather than adding code.

## User Impact

Operators and agents talking to self-hosted or misbehaving Mattermost servers get a clean, accurate API error (`Mattermost API 503 Service Unavailable: unknown error`) on null-body JSON error responses, instead of an internal `Unexpected end of JSON input` crash — and every error read now goes through the bounded reader.

## Evidence

**1. Real-behavior proof with a negative control (real HTTP).** A harness drives the **real exported** `readMattermostError` and the **real** `createMattermostClient().request()` (through the real `fetchWithSsrFGuard` SSRF path, `allowPrivateNetwork: true` for localhost) against a real `node:http` server returning `503 Service Unavailable` with `content-type: application/json` and **no body**. The negative control runs the deleted branch's exact logic (`await res.json()`) on the same response.

```
$ tsx mm-proof.mts
Server returns 503 Service Unavailable, content-type application/json, NO body

[NEGATIVE CONTROL] old `!res.body` json branch: await res.json()
PASS: old branch crashes on null-body JSON error — THREW SyntaxError: Unexpected end of JSON input

[WITH FIX] readMattermostError(real null-body 503 json response)
PASS: returns empty string, no throw — detail=""

[WITH FIX] createMattermostClient().request() against null-body 503
PASS: clean bounded API error (no JSON crash) — "Mattermost API 503 Service Unavailable: unknown error"

ALL PROOF ASSERTIONS PASSED
```

Without the fix the null-body JSON error path throws `SyntaxError: Unexpected end of JSON input`; with the fix `readMattermostError` returns `""` and the client surfaces the clean `... 503 Service Unavailable: unknown error`.

**2. Committed regression tests** (`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/client.test.ts`) cover: null-body JSON errors never call `response.json()` / `response.text()`; bounded JSON `message` fields still parse from normal bodies; and a guarded null-body failure surfaced end-to-end through `createMattermostClient().request()`.

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/client.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  27 passed (27)
```

**3. Lint on the changed file:**

```
$ node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/client.ts   # exit 0
```

**What was NOT tested:** I did not run against a live production Mattermost server. The null-body JSON error condition is reproduced with a real local HTTP server over the real SSRF-guarded fetch path, including the negative control showing the pre-fix crash.

AI-assisted: implementation, tests, and the proof harness were drafted with agent assistance; all commands above were run locally.
